risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomisation is undertaken by central allocation using a computerized random number generator... randomisation is performed to assign patients... via the minimisation method." Comment: This describes a clearly random allocation process with computerized random number generation, central allocation, and minimisation with stratification, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "To avoid biased allocation, randomisation is undertaken by central allocation using a computerized random number generator at the Internet Data and Information Center for Medical Research (INDICE), provided by the University Hospital Medical Information Network (UMIN) in Japan. After written informed consent has been obtained, randomisation is performed to assign patients..." Comment: This explicitly describes central allocation, which prevents the enrolling staff from foreseeing the next assignment; therefore allocation concealment is adequate.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the study, neither the patient nor staff can be blinded." Comment: This is an open-label intervention, so participants and personnel delivering the intervention knew allocation. The main outcomes include patient satisfaction and attitudes toward medication, which could plausibly be influenced by awareness of group assignment, so the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Quote: "Due to the nature of the study, neither the patient nor staff can be blinded." Comment: The primary outcome (CSQ-8J treatment satisfaction) and secondary outcome DAI-10 are self-report measures, so the patient is the outcome assessor; because participants were explicitly not blinded, knowledge of allocation could directly influence these subjective ratings. The BPRS symptom severity was assessed by the ward doctor, who was also not blinded, and this is a clinician-judgement outcome that could also be influenced by allocation knowledge. Quote: "Treatment continuation is assessed from their medical records..." Comment: although this outcome is more objective, the study includes clearly subjective and judgement-dependent outcomes assessed without blinding, so detection bias is high overall for this domain.

Quote: "The data will be analysed on an intention-to-treat principle. Multiple imputation methods [28] will be used for imputing any missing information regarding outcome measures." Comment: The study planned ITT analysis with multiple imputation for missing outcome data, which is an appropriate and plausible method for handling attrition. The report also anticipated only 10% dropout and does not describe any substantial or clearly imbalanced loss to follow-up that would suggest attrition bias.
